primary_diagnosis.name: Clinically isolated syndrome — acute optic neuritis with facial sensory symptoms (demyelinating), stroke not excludable remotely

pathophysiology_summary: A clinically isolated syndrome is a first demyelinating event; optic neuritis produces subacute monocular visual loss with painful eye movements, central scotoma, colour (red) desaturation and a relative afferent pupillary defect, often with other CNS sensory symptoms. Although the underlying process here is inflammatory and usually self-limiting, the PRESENTATION — an acute focal neurological deficit evolving over hours — overlaps with ischaemic stroke/TIA (including posterior circulation events causing visual and sensory symptoms). A remote clinician cannot perform the examination (RAPD, visual fields, cranial nerves, cerebellar signs) or obtain urgent neuroimaging needed to exclude stroke within the thrombolysis window. Safe practice is urgent in-person assessment; the demyelination diagnosis is confirmed later with MRI brain/orbits and neurology review.

differential.evidence_basis: Acute focal neurological deficit within hours; must be excluded urgently and cannot be excluded remotely.
Painful eye movements, central blur and colour desaturation strongly suggest optic neuritis, but this remains a clinical suspicion pending examination and imaging.
Can cause visual and sensory symptoms, but the subacute continuous monocular loss with painful eye movements is atypical.
No facial motor weakness; sensory tingling is not Bell's palsy.

Painful eye movements, central blur, colour desaturation (DI-003) with no motor/speech/cerebellar deficit (DI-002) favour optic neuritis — but confirmation and stroke exclusion require in-person assessment and imaging.
Remains a can't-miss that cannot be excluded remotely; the absence of limb/speech signs reduces but does not eliminate posterior-circulation stroke.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output is URGENT in-person assessment (ED/acute neurology) to exclude stroke and obtain timely imaging — not remote reassurance with an outpatient MRI in 48 h. Recognising optic neuritis is good, but must not down-triage the acute deficit.

red_flags.description: Acute focal neurological deficit of sudden onset within the stroke window
red_flags.clinical_significance: Cannot exclude stroke/TIA remotely; time-critical for reperfusion if ischaemic.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed acute stroke loses the reperfusion window, causing permanent disability.

red_flags.description: Progression to limb weakness, speech disturbance, facial droop, or ataxia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would strongly indicate stroke and mandate emergency ambulance.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed recognition of an evolving stroke.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Diagnosing optic neuritis/MS remotely and arranging outpatient MRI, thereby down-triaging an acute focal deficit that could be a stroke.
Assuming a young anxious patient cannot be having a stroke and failing to escalate.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Relative afferent pupillary defect, visual fields and acuity
Full cranial-nerve, motor, sensory and cerebellar examination
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Urgent in-person assessment (ED/acute neurology) today to exclude stroke and image the brain/orbits; call 000 if stroke features progress.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Labelling this as optic neuritis remotely and deferring to outpatient MRI risks missing an acute stroke within the treatable window.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Face/Arm/Speech currently negative but Time-critical focal deficit present
risk_scores.clinical_implication: A negative FAST does not exclude posterior-circulation stroke; acute focal deficit warrants urgent in-person assessment regardless.
